A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: AIRE — Autoimmune Regulator — Promotes tissue-restricted antigen (TRA) expression in mTECs; Mutation → APS-1 (APECED); FEZF2 — TRA expression in mTECs (Complementary to AIRE); BIM (BCL2L11) — Mediates apoptosis during negative selection. What is the most likely diagnosis?
In a patient with suspected Systemic Autoimmune Diseases, which of the following findings would be most characteristic?

A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: Joints — Symmetrical polyarthritis (MCP, PIP, Wrist), Morning stiffness >1h, Synovitis; Extra-articular — Rheumatoid nodules, Rheumatoid vasculitis, Felty's syndrome, Interstitial lung disease, Pericarditis, Amyloidosis; Systemic — Fatigue, Low-grade fever, Weight loss, Anaemia of chronic disease. What is the most likely diagnosis?
In a patient with suspected Autoimmune Diagnostics, which of the following findings would be most characteristic?

A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: A[Hypersensitivity Reactions] --> B[Type I: IgE-Mediated (Immediate)]; A --> C[Type II: Antibody-Mediated (Cytotoxic)]; A --> D[Type III: Immune Complex-Mediated]. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: Hyperacute — Pre-formed DSA → Complement activation → Immediate thrombosis, Necrosis; Acute Cellular Rejection (ACR) — Host T cells → Donor HLA (Direct > Indirect); Antibody-Mediated Rejection (AMR) — Donor-Specific Antibodies (DSA) → Complement Activation, Endothelial Injury. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: PD-1 — PD-L1 (B7-H1), PD-L2; CTLA-4 — CD80 (B7-1), CD86 (B7-2); LAG-3 (CD223) — MHC Class II, FGL1, LSECtin. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: Adverse Effect — Incidence; TB Reactivation — 1-5% (Highest with Infliximab/Adalimumab); Serious Infections — 3-5%. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A patient with suspected Clinical Immunology presents with: Vaccination — Reduced Response to ALL Vaccines; Higher Doses/Adjuvants Needed (High-Dose Flu, Adjuvanted Shingrix, High-Dose Hep B); Repeated Doses; Infections — ↑ Respiratory Infections (Influenza, Pneumococcal, COVID-19, RSV), Zoster Reactivation, UTIs, Sepsis; Cancer — ↓ Immune Surveillance → ↑ Incidence, ↓ Immunotherapy Efficacy (Checkpoint Inhibitors). What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Ferrous sulphate 200 mg BD + vitamin C; investigate menorrhagia

options.B: Therapeutic phlebotomy (450 mL weekly until ferritin <50)

question: A 60-year-old man on chronic haemodialysis has Hb 9 g/dL, ferritin 600, transferrin saturation 18%. Most appropriate management?

options.B: IV iron (e.g., ferric carboxymaltose) + ESA (epoetin alfa/darbepoetin)

question: A 30-year-old woman with severe IDA (Hb 7, ferritin 5) fails to respond to oral iron 200 mg BD. She has coeliac disease. Best next step?

options.C: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single infusion)

options.C: Switch to low FODMAP diet + psyllium 5 g/day; avoid bran/inulin/FOS

question: A 60-year-old man with T2DM (HbA1c 8.0%) on metformin wishes to add fibre for glycaemic control. Best recommendation?

options.B: Psyllium 5 g with each main meal (15 g/day)

question: A 72-year-old man with diverticulosis (2 prior diverticulitis episodes) asks how to prevent recurrence. Evidence-based advice?

options.C: High fibre >25 g/day (insoluble + soluble); nuts/seeds not contraindicated

question: A 55-year-old woman on atorvastatin 20 mg has LDL-C 3.2 mmol/L. She wants dietary adjunct. Best single fibre?

options.B: β-glucan 3 g/day (70 g rolled oats) OR psyllium 7–10 g/day

question: A 65-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 28), constipated, K+ 5.3 mmol/L. Safest fibre supplement?

options.B: Psyllium 5 g BD (low K+, no mineral binding)

question: A 52-year-old man presents with eruptive xanthomata on his back and buttocks. Triglycerides 18.2 mmol/L, cholesterol 12.4 mmol/L. He consumes 60 g alcohol daily. Most appropriate immediate management?

options.C: NPO, IV fluid resuscitation, insulin infusion, alcohol cessation

options.C: Direct LDL-C assay or Martin/Hopkins equation

options.B: Stop isotretinoin immediately; refer for fetal anomaly scan; counsel teratogenic risk (Category X)

question: An 18-month-old child presents with inability to see in dim light and foamy triangular deposits on temporal conjunctivae. Weight 8 kg. Next step?

options.B: Vitamin A 200,000 IU oral Day 1, Day 2, Day 14

question: A 45-year-old man taking vitamin A supplement 50,000 IU daily for 2 years presents with bone pain, dry skin, and headache. Fundoscopy shows papilloedema. Likely diagnosis?

options.B: Chronic hypervitaminosis A with pseudotumor cerebri

question: A pregnant woman at 12 weeks gestation asks about vitamin A supplementation for night blindness. Safe upper limit?
A 6-month-old infant with cystic fibrosis has steatorrhea and poor weight gain. Which fat-soluble vitamin requires water-miscible preparation?

options.C: Vitamin A (and D, E, K all require water-miscible forms)

question: A 2-year-old child presents with bowed legs, rachitic rosary, and ALP 950 U/L. 25(OH)D 9 ng/mL. Best initial treatment?

options.B: Vitamin D3 2000 IU/day (or 50,000 IU weekly) ×6 weeks + Calcium 500 mg/day

question: A 60-year-old woman with hijab, bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones on pubic rami. 25(OH)D 6 ng/mL. Management?

options.C: Cholecalciferol 50,000 IU weekly ×8 weeks → maintenance 2000 IU/day + Calcium 1000 mg/day

question: A 10-year-old girl with short stature, genu valgum, normal calcium, low phosphate, normal ALP, 25(OH)D 32 ng/mL. Father affected. Genetic basis?
A 65-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22), secondary hyperparathyroidism (PTH 320), 25(OH)D 18 ng/mL. Best vitamin D therapy?
A 40-year-old woman with sarcoidosis presents with hypercalcaemia (Ca 12.2 mg/dL), normal PTH, elevated 1,25(OH)2D, normal 25(OH)D. Cause?
A 6-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis presents with progressive gait ataxia, loss of vibration sense, and night blindness. Plasma α-tocopherol 1.5 µmol/L (ref 11–37). Best management?

options.B: Water-miscible vitamin E 400 IU/day; titrate to plasma α-tocopherol >20 µmol/L

question: A 14-year-old girl presents with progressive ataxia, areflexia, and retinopathy. Lipid profile normal. Plasma α-tocopherol 0.6 µmol/L. Genetic testing confirms TTPA mutation. Treatment?
A 28-week preterm infant develops haemolytic anaemia (Hb 7 g/dL, reticulocytes 15%, bilirubin 8 mg/dL). Vitamin E level low. Prevention in NICU?
A 65-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation starts vitamin E 400 IU/day for "heart health." INR rises from 2.5 to 4.2. Mechanism?

options.A: Vitamin E inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase
options.B: Vitamin E has antiplatelet effect + may potentiate warfarin

question: A 55-year-old man on simvastatin 40 mg + extended-release niacin 2 g for low HDL-C considers adding vitamin E 400 IU. Why should this be avoided?

options.B: Vitamin E + vitamin C + β-carotene blunt the HDL₂ rise from niacin-statin combination

question: A 3-week-old exclusively breastfed infant with biliary atresia presents with intracerebral haemorrhage. INR 5.2. Not given vitamin K at birth. Immediate management?

options.B: Phytomenadione 1 mg IV + cranial imaging + neurosurgical consultation

question: A 70-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation (INR target 2–3) starts clarithromycin for pneumonia. On day 4, INR is 5.8. He has no bleeding. Best action?

options.A: Stop warfarin, give phytomenadione 5 mg IV
options.B: Hold warfarin 1–2 doses, give phytomenadione 1–2 mg PO, monitor INR daily

question: A 65-year-old woman on warfarin (mechanical mitral valve, INR target 2.5–3.5) presents with large retroperitoneal haematoma. INR 8.2. Most appropriate immediate reversal?

An 80-year-old man with Crohn's disease on prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics develops bruising and haematuria. Not on anticoagulants. PT 22s (control 12s), aPTT normal, platelets normal. Diagnosis?

options.B: Vitamin K deficiency (malabsorption + antibiotic suppression of gut flora)

options.A: Stop INH, switch to alternative TB regimen

question: A 6-month-old infant on TPN without biotin develops scaly periorificial rash, alopecia, conjunctivitis, and metabolic acidosis. Urinary organic acids show elevated 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid. Diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman takes pyridoxine 500 mg daily for "premenstrual syndrome" for 2 years. She develops progressive sensory ataxia, loss of vibration sense, and pseudoathetosis. Nerve conduction studies show absent sensory potentials, normal motor. Diagnosis?
A mother of a 4-month-old exclusively breastfed infant consumes 6 raw egg whites daily in "protein shakes." Infant develops scaly rash around eyes/mouth, alopecia, and conjunctivitis. Cause?

options.B: Biotin deficiency (avidin in raw egg whites via breast milk)

question: A 55-year-old alcoholic presents with burning feet, fatigue, and insomnia. No neurological deficits. Dietary history reveals minimal intake of all food groups. Which vitamin deficiency is classically described but virtually never seen in isolation?
A 12-year-old boy presents with episodic cerebellar ataxia, nystagmus, and a photosensitive rash on sun-exposed areas. Urinary neutral amino acids increased. Diagnosis?
A 55-year-old man with metastatic carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea) develops a photosensitive rash on his neck and hands, glossitis, and confusion. Mechanism of niacin deficiency?
A 30-year-old man on isoniazid for pulmonary TB develops peripheral neuropathy and a photosensitive rash. Prevention strategy?
An alcoholic patient presents with cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, and superficial corneal vascularisation. Most likely deficiency?
A patient with pellagra is started on nicotinic acid 500 mg TDS. He develops intense facial flushing and pruritus. Prophylaxis for flushing?
A 40-year-old man with alcohol use disorder is brought in confused, with horizontal nystagmus and broad-based ataxic gait. Blood glucose 16 mmol/L. Immediate management?

options.B: IV thiamine 500 mg STAT, THEN manage glucose; continue 500 mg TDS ×3–5d

question: A 28-year-old woman at 10 weeks gestation with hyperemesis gravidarum has received IV dextrose for 3 days. She develops nystagmus and confusion. Next step?

options.B: STOP dextrose; IV thiamine 500 mg STAT; Mg²⁺ repletion; continue TDS ×3–5d

question: A 55-year-old man 6 months post-RYGB presents with burning feet, glove-stocking sensory loss, and foot drop. Most likely deficiency?
A 6-month-old exclusively breastfed infant presents with tachycardia, oedema, cardiomegaly, and aphonia. Mother has poor diet. Diagnosis?
A 70-year-old man on high-dose furosemide 160 mg/day for heart failure develops new tachycardia, peripheral oedema, and lactic acidosis. Echocardiogram shows hyperdynamic LV. Likely cause?

A 70-year-old woman presents with fatigue, macrocytic anaemia (Hb 8.5, MCV 112), and loss of vibration sense in both feet. B12 is 80 pmol/L, MMA 2.5 µmol/L, anti-intrinsic factor antibody positive. Most appropriate treatment?

options.B: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM alternate days × 5, then 1 mg IM every 3 months lifelong

question: A 50-year-old man with pernicious anaemia on long-term B12 IM therapy develops increasing fatigue 1 year after switching to oral cyanocobalamin 50 µg daily. B12 level 90 pmol/L. Most likely cause?

options.B: Insufficient oral dose (need 1–2 mg/day for passive absorption)

question: A 30-year-old woman presents with loss of vibration and proprioception, spastic paraparesis, and macrocytic anaemia. She is a strict vegan. B12 100 pmol/L, MMA 2.0 µmol/L, homocysteine 50 µmol/L. Most appropriate management?

options.B: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM alternate days × 5, then dietary + oral B12 50–100 µg/day

question: A 25-year-old who is a heavy user of nitrous oxide recreationally presents with progressive lower limb sensory loss and gait ataxia. Serum B12 is 250 pmol/L (normal). Next best step?

options.B: Check MMA (likely elevated) and start hydroxocobalamin IM; N₂O cessation

question: A 55-year-old with type 2 diabetes on metformin 2 g/day, Hb 9 g/dL, MCV 100, B12 130 pmol/L, asymptomatic. Best management?

options.C: Oral cyanocobalamin 50–100 µg/day; consider metformin dose reduction

question: A 60-year-old alcoholic man presents with bleeding, swollen gums, corkscrew hairs on the abdomen, and perifollicular haemorrhages on the lower extremities. Plasma ascorbate 8 µmol/L. Best treatment?

options.B: Vitamin C 300 mg PO daily ×1 month

question: A 30-year-old woman is being investigated for iron deficiency anaemia. She has heavy menstrual periods and is advised to take iron supplements. What cofactor enhances iron absorption?
A 65-year-old man with hereditary haemochromatosis is considering supplementation with high-dose vitamin C. What is the concern?

options.B: Increased iron absorption and tissue iron deposition

question: A 50-year-old smoker with gum bleeding and recurrent infections. Smoker (40 pack-years). What is his additional vitamin C requirement beyond the standard RDA?
A 6-month-old infant on cow's milk (not breastfed) presents with irritability, pseudoparalysis of the leg, painful swelling of the thigh, and scorbutic rosary. Most likely diagnosis?
A 40-year-old woman 24h after total thyroidectomy develops perioral numbness and carpopedal spasm. QT prolonged on ECG. Most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.B: IV calcium gluconate 10% 1 g in 50 mL D5W over 10 min

question: A 65-year-old man with CKD stage 4 has PO4 2.5 mmol/L, Ca 2.0, PTH 600. Best initial treatment?
